title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 39:1-6 When Joseph was taken to Egypt by the Ishmaelite traders, he was purchased by Potiphar, an Egyptian officer. Potiphar was captain of the guard for Pharaoh, the king of Egypt. The LORD was with Joseph, so he succeeded in everything he did as he served in the home of his Egyptian master. Potiphar noticed this and realized that the LORD was with Joseph, giving him success in everything he did. This pleased Potiphar, so he soon made Joseph his personal attendant. He put him in charge of his entire household and everything he owned. From the day Joseph was put in charge of his master’s household and property, the LORD began to bless Potiphar’s household for Joseph’s sake. All his household affairs ran smoothly, and his crops and livestock flourished. So Potiphar gave Joseph complete administrative responsibility over everything he owned. With Joseph there, he didn’t worry about a thing — except what kind of food to eat!
weekTheme: Boys complain, men contribute This week, we will look at something every young man has to learn how to handle: the difficulty of circumstances. Throughout our lives, we will all find ourselves in situations that we hate and doing things we don't like. Men don't waste time and energy wishing things were different or complaining and pouting. They ask God where he is working, what they can do to make the most of the situation, and get busy with what is in front of them.

days.title: Possibility
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:1 When Joseph was taken to Egypt by the Ishmaelite traders, he was purchased by Potiphar, an Egyptian officer.
days.reflection: As a young man, you are going to find yourself in situations that you hate. There is no way around it. But how you handle these circumstances is going to define the kind of man that you become. Most people have a default mode of complaining and whining. But as good as this may feel, it doesn't really work to change anything. If someone had a right to complain about his circumstances, it would be Joseph. He finds himself in the worst place a teenager can imagine. First, he is in Egypt. Not because he wants to be there. And not because he wants to know about hieroglyphics or swim in the Nile river. He has been taken there to be a slave. He is in a place where he doesn't speak the language, doesn't know the customs, does not have any friends or any support. He has been taken to Egypt. Second, he has been purchased. He belongs to someone else and has lost the right to live how he wants. He is going to have to serve someone else's desires. His time, energy, strength and focus will all be given away, and there is nothing that he can do about it. What a beatdown! Can you imagine how he must be feeling at this point? He probably longed to be home with his Father where he was the favored son. Longed to put his coat on and walk around feeling special. Long for the familiarity of his own home, people and place. BUT - There is something amazing in this story that we see in Joseph's life. There isn't a single mention of him complaining or whining. He simply accepts what has happened, trusts and obeys God where he is, and waits for his story to unfold. How on earth is this possible? How can someone handle such a horrible situation like this? The book of James in the New Testament gives us a clue. It says this: "Dear brothers and sisters, when troubles of any kind come your way, consider it an opportunity for great joy. For you know that when your faith is tested, your endurance has a chance to grow. So let it grow, for when your endurance is fully developed, you will be perfect and complete, needing nothing.” James 1:2-4. God can turn the circumstances we hate into places we grow. Almost like training on a sports team or going to the gym. If we choose to have the right attitude in spite of what we see, the Bible says we grow to become mature and complete, needing nothing. Imagine being a man like that! Imagine being a man who has the persevering power to handle any and all circumstances that come along. A man that is able to handle hard classes, able to handle hard friendships, able to handle criticism, able to handle gossip coming against you, able to handles setbacks. That's what God did in Joseph, and that's what he wants to do in you. As Paul said to the Philippians, you can do all things through him who gives you strength.
questions: Have you ever been in a situation that you really couldn't stand, but afterwards saw that you were better because of it? What was it, what did you learn?

If you have played on a sports team, how did you feel about doing all the practices? Why did you think it was worth it? How can you see hard circumstances as a kind of practice for larger challenges in life?
Take a moment to think about your least favorite situation in your life right now. What is it? Brainstorm 2 or 3 ways that you can reframe the situation as a place of possibility and growth.

thoughts.verse: Do everything without complaining and arguing, so that no one can criticize you. Live clean, innocent lives as children of God, shining like bright lights in a world full of crooked and perverse people.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a new perspective about the hard circumstances you may be facing at the moment. Ask him to show you how he may be making you more mature and strong as a result of it.

days.title: Providence
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:1 When Joseph was taken to Egypt by the Ishmaelite traders, he was purchased by Potiphar, an Egyptian officer. Potiphar was captain of the guard for Pharaoh, the king of Egypt.
days.reflection: Every young man has to answer the question about who or what he believes is guiding and directing the circumstances of his life. Are my circumstances created by my own choices? Are they created by the choices of others? Does God play any role in the circumstance I find myself in? This is one of the most important questions a young man must settle in his heart, because they will determine the level of hope for the future he finds when things are hard. If life is only your choices, you will feel overwhelmed with shame when you make bad ones. If life is only other people's choices, you will feel powerless and trapped. But if God is somehow working in our circumstances in spite of our choices and the choices of others, it means that there is no place we will find ourselves that is beyond the reach of God's kindness and purpose. Christians call this idea of God working in our lives the providence of God. That sounds like a big, formal word, but it simply means God is directing and shaping our lives, even if we can't see it. He is at work in everything, even when his plans and purposes may be hidden. We see a really hopeful example of this in the circumstances Joseph finds himself in. Even though he is in slavery in Egypt, God is working through it all. How can we tell? Joseph has ended up with Potiphar, who works directly for Pharaoh. This is going to play a key role in the revelation of his destiny later on, though Joseph can't see any of it right now. Think about it: Joseph could have been bought by any number of people, and lived a life we have never heard about. He could have lived on the edge of the empire, working for a grumpy old man, and just did that until he died. End of story. But Joseph is being positioned by God for a much greater plan, even if he can't see it in his current circumstances. God is working in the circumstances of your life too. He has planned your life out before the world even began, and he has put you here, now, because he has a purpose and destiny for you. Ephesians 2:10 says, “For we are God’s masterpiece. He has created us anew in Christ Jesus, so we can do the good things he planned for us long ago.” How cool is that? Your life is not an accident! God is working in your most challenging circumstances, and he will bring good out of even the most painful and hard things. You live in a world where you are not left alone to chance, or luck, or any other force. You are in a great story God is telling and you're a part of it, even if you can't see it from your current spot.
questions: Knowing what you do about Joseph, what would you say to him at this moment in his life if you had the chance to talk to him? How would you give him hope or encourage him?
If Joseph was able to come to this meeting right now, what do you think he would say to you about the challenges you are facing?
Ask the person you are doing this discussion with to share a time when he saw God work in a powerful way, even when he couldn't see it at the time.

thoughts.verse: And we know that God causes everything to work together for the good of those who love God and are called according to his purpose for them.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he has a plan and purpose for your life. Ask him to show you some clues of what he may be up to in the midst of your current difficult situation.

days.title: Presence
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:2-4 2 The LORD was with Joseph, so he succeeded in everything he did as he served in the home of his Egyptian master. 3 Potiphar noticed this and realized that the LORD was with Joseph, giving him success in everything he did. 4 This pleased Potiphar, so he soon made Joseph his personal attendant. He put him in charge of his entire household and everything he owned.
days.reflection: One of the hardest things about being in circumstances and situations we hate is the fact that we feel so alone. Stuck, hating it, lonely, frustrated. Alone. But if someone is a Christian, we actually have an amazing source of help. In fact, we have a real advantage because a Christian is never really alone. The difference between terrible circumstances for christians and non-christians is this: God is always with us no matter what we go through. You can see this really clearly in this stage of Joseph's life. In just five words, we see a completely different perspective of what is happening in this story. Read this next sentence slowly: THE LORD WAS WITH JOSEPH. Wild! What a difference. Joseph was still in Egypt. He was still a slave. He was still stuck. But he was not alone. Joseph was not abandoned to random fate. God was by his side. When he felt lonely, God was with him. When he was afraid, God was with him. When he felt sad, God was with him. In fact, it was so obvious that God was with him that the man who bought him had to acknowledge it. The pagan Potiphar realized “The Lord was with Joseph.” You may have moments where things seem so discouraging and so hopeless, but in those moments, learn to calm down and observe what is happening at a deeper level. If you train your eyes and tune your ears you can begin to discern something amazing in the midst of the problems - the presence of God. The presence of God can show up in some weird and cool ways. Sometimes it's like a surprising strength inside of you when you feel like you can't go on. Sometimes it's like a feeling of hope when you just want to give up. Sometimes it's a strange feeling of being loved when people are picking on you. These things aren't random. This is God letting you know you are not alone. The Apostle Timothy faced some horrible circumstances at the end of his life. His friends ran off and left him when things got hard, and he was stuck in some nasty circumstance. But at the moment when all hope was lost, he experienced something amazing. He writes: "The first time I was brought before the judge, no one came with me. Everyone abandoned me. May it not be counted against them. But the Lord stood with me and gave me strength so that I might preach the Good News in its entirety for all the Gentiles to hear. And he rescued me from certain death. Yes, and the Lord will deliver me from every evil attack and will bring me safely into his heavenly Kingdom. All glory to God forever and ever! Amen." 2 Timothy 4:16-18 God will be with you wherever you go and no matter what you face. He will stand by your side too. Jesus promised in Matthew 28 that “He would be with us always, even to the ends of the earth.” That includes your school, home, locker room, friend's houses, church, everywhere. You are never alone.
questions: Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they sensed the presence of God in their life. Where was it, and how did they know God was with them?
What is one of the loneliest moments you can remember from this last year?
Have you ever had a sense of God's presence? Maybe at church, or at a camp, or in the middle of doing something you love? If so, what did it feel like?

thoughts.verse: Do not be afraid or discouraged, for the LORD will personally go ahead of you. He will be with you; he will neither fail you nor abandon you.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to still your heart before God. Ask him to begin to teach you to know his presence in your life.

days.title: Preparation
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:2-5 2 The LORD was with Joseph, so he succeeded in everything he did as he served in the home of his Egyptian master. 3 Potiphar noticed this and realized that the LORD was with Joseph, giving him success in everything he did. 4 This pleased Potiphar, so he soon made Joseph his personal attendant. He put him in charge of his entire household and everything he owned. 5 From the day Joseph was put in charge of his master’s household and property, the LORD began to bless Potiphar’s household for Joseph’s sake. All his household affairs ran smoothly, and his crops and livestock flourished.
days.reflection: The reason it is important to respond with the right perspective in bad circumstances is because our circumstances can prepare us for our future. You can learn skills, mental toughness, an overcoming spirit, and how to walk with God. These hard times can make you the kind of man God can use in the future. God is getting you ready for something later on, and there are clues for what that may be all around you. Because God is with Joseph, Potiphar puts him in charge of his entire household and everything he owns. Joseph is young, but he is trustworthy. Joseph is learning how to be a manager. Joseph is learning about responsibility. Joseph is learning leadership. Though I am sure Joseph wishes he could learn these lessons in his father's house, he is still getting the skills and preparation he needs for what God has in store for him later. Joseph teaches us that you don't need good circumstances to be a good man. This is good news for you. Your preparation begins right where you are. God has prepared people for their future in some of the weirdest and worst places. David was a shepherd hanging out with sheep in the middle of nowhere while God prepared him. John the Baptist was hanging out in the wilderness while God prepared him. Abraham was living in the midst of really ungodly people when God prepared him. God even worked to prepare Paul when he hated Christians. His life was shaped by things that would matter after he met Jesus. And God prepared Jesus to be the savior of the world by doing manual labor in a carpenter's shop. God will use hard times to get us ready for what he has for us. If you talk to any adult, they will tell you that the hard times of their teenage years taught them many valuable lessons that shaped them into the adults they are. What may God be preparing you for? What could he have in store for your future that you can start getting ready for now. If you pay attention, you may get some clues, but if you complain and whine, you may never know. Bad circumstances can lead to good things if you learn to think about preparation in your current circumstances.
questions: Even though Joseph was in terrible circumstances, he was able to learn key lessons that would help him later in his life. Can you list 1 or 2 things you may have learned in hard times you have been through that have helped you at a later point?

Joseph was put in charge of Potiphar’s entire household. What sort of skills do you think he had to develop to be able to run all of that? Take a moment and list them out. How do you think skills like this could help Joseph in the future?
Are there any skills or lessons that you wish you could learn now to help you in the future? What are they, and why do you want to learn them?

thoughts.verse: Then he sent someone to Egypt ahead of them — Joseph, who was sold as a slave. They bruised his feet with fetters and placed his neck in an iron collar. Until the time came to fulfill his dreams, the LORD tested Joseph’s character.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to show you the lessons he is trying to teach you and the skills he is trying to hone in your life. Ask him to show you ways he may be preparing you for your future.

days.title: Prosperity
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:2-6 2 The LORD was with Joseph, so he succeeded in everything he did as he served in the home of his Egyptian master. 3 Potiphar noticed this and realized that the LORD was with Joseph, giving him success in everything he did. 4 This pleased Potiphar, so he soon made Joseph his personal attendant. He put him in charge of his entire household and everything he owned. 5 From the day Joseph was put in charge of his master’s household and property, the LORD began to bless Potiphar’s household for Joseph’s sake. All his household affairs ran smoothly, and his crops and livestock flourished. 6 So Potiphar gave Joseph complete administrative responsibility over everything he owned. With Joseph there, he didn’t worry about a thing—except what kind of food to eat!
days.reflection: Because God is in control of our lives, he is able to make us happy in horrible places and bless us when things are a beatdown. Even though Joseph is in a really tough season with circumstances he probably hates, this time is not wasted. God is working. God is using this season to shape Joseph's character, develop his skills, draw him closer to himself. This is a season of real maturity for Joseph. He is learning to deal with all kinds of difficulty, and it is transforming him from a boy into a man. God is so kind to Joseph that he doesn’t just comfort him with his presence, he rewards him with his blessing. Look at the words used to describe Joseph's work. “The Lord gave him success in all he did.” “the LORD began to bless Potiphar’s household for Joseph’s sake.” “his crops and livestock flourished.” God has made Joseph successful. God is making the whole household successful. Even the cattle in the field and the crops and fruit are blessed because Joseph has God's hand and favor on him. Imagine living in that house, and everything is all of a sudden becoming amazing. Cash comes in, the farm is growing fruit like you have never seen and everything is simply getting better. Everyone wants to know what has happened? Why is this happening? Where is this coming from? And when people figure it out, they realize it's because one of the new slaves is being faithful to God, overcoming challenging circumstances, and using those circumstances to grow. It is God, not our circumstances that determine our success. It is God, not our leaders, employer, teachers or coaches who determine our success. It is God who has the power to make the things we work for work out well. God can bless you where you are too. He can help you navigate the challenging circumstances that you face. He can bless you, cause the things you are working on to do really well, and give favor to those around you. But God will only do it for people who really care about him and are loyal to him no matter what. If you are going to move away from becoming a boy and become a man, you are going to have to learn to deal with the difficulty of circumstances. You will have to view them as an opportunity for growth. Do you want to be a man like Joseph who God can trust and God can bless?
questions: When you hear the word blessing, what is the first thing that comes to your mind?
According to your definition of what blessing is, are there any areas of your life that you can see that God has blessed you, just like he did with Joseph?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when God blessed them. What was the blessing, how did they know it was God, what was the result of it?

thoughts.verse: Oh, the joys of those who do not follow the advice of the wicked, or stand around with sinners, or join in with mockers. But they delight in the law of the LORD, meditating on it day and night. They are like trees planted along the riverbank, bearing fruit each season. Their leaves never wither, and they prosper in all they do.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to bless you where you are right now. Ask him to give you power to overcome the difficulty of your circumstances and become a young man that God can trust.

extras: WEEK 3 MANSCHOOL PAYING ATTENTION TO THE CLUES AROUND YOU MAN SCHOOL REMINDER: "The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner." - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ESCAPE ROOM If you have ever done an escape room, you know how important it is to pay attention to the small details so you can find what is happening in the place you are stuck. This is a great way to illustrate what Joseph had to do in the difficulty of circumstances. He was faithful and diligent and trusting that God was working in ways around him, preparing and training him. Use this to illustrate how to find clues for a way out when we feel stuck in bad circumstances. Make sure it’s an escape room you can get out of. Maybe one you have done before so you can guide him. Or bring some friends along and carefully explain how an escape room works in advance so they are prepared to find the clues and get after it. OPTION 2. WATCH THE KARATE KID Have a discussion after the film about how he was trained and prepared (Wax on, Wax off) in ordinary circumstances for his future, even when he couldn’t see it at the time. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out for a meal at a place he loves, and do a recap of the week, sharing your own experiences around the theme of the week and the core ideas (Overcoming the difficulty of circumstances and Possibility, Presence, Providence, Preparation, Prosperity). Just work on building the emotional bond, listening, and drawing out his heart.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
